repairBuyOrReplace.h2: G‑Class G65 AMG — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Replace all twelve coils as a set — prevent further issues
Viable repair — catch before turbo damage occurs
Essential maintenance — catch before catastrophic failure
Piston‑valve contact means little is left to repair
Preventive maintenance — replace hoses and coolant
Labour‑intensive but essential — prevent oil loss

buyingChecks.title: Buying This G65 AMG? Check These First
buyIf: * Ignition coils replaced recently (full set of twelve) — heat exposure is the main killer
* Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (critical for timing chain longevity)
* No cold‑start rattling (timing chain tensioner health)
* Turbo bypass valves functioning correctly (no boost loss or unusual noises)
* Cooling system recently serviced (hoses, coolant, water pump)
* Post‑2015 model with the 630 PS upgrade
avoidIf: * Misfires or rough running — ignition coils may be failing (£2,000 repair)
* Cold‑start rattling and no timing chain history — budget for chain work
* No service history — the M279 is unforgiving of neglect
* Unusual turbo noises or power loss — bypass valves or turbo issues likely
* Oil leaks or coolant loss — seals and cooling system may be degrading
* Any history of ignition or turbo issues without documented repairs

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The G65 AMG is the pinnacle of the W463 G‑Class — a handcrafted 6.0‑litre V12 biturbo SUV producing up to 630 hp and 738 lb‑ft of torque. With only 28,000 miles on a 2016 example, it remains a rare and desirable machine. The M279 V12 is derived from the legendary M275 family that powered the S 65 AMG, CL 65 AMG, and even the Pagani Huayra. However, the engine generates immense heat from the twin‑turbochargers, and ignition coils are a known weakness — the V12 has twelve individual coils that can fail due to heat exposure. Other concerns include turbo bypass valve leaks, timing chain tensioner wear, and cooling system degradation. With typical G65 AMG values ranging from £60,000 to £120,000+, an engine replacement is a major financial commitment — but the G65 AMG is a collector's piece that rewards careful ownership. A well‑maintained example with documented ignition coil and cooling system history is the safest choice.

cta.label: → Compare G65 AMG engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz G‑Class G65 AMG (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Ignition System — Check for smooth idle; any misfires may indicate coil failure — see the map above
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering (timing chain tensioner warning)
* Turbo Health — Test acceleration; any lag, "whirring" noises, or power loss indicates bypass valve or turbo issues
* Service History — Including regular oil changes (critical for timing chain longevity)
* Cooling System — Check for coolant leaks, sweet smells, or overheating — V12 generates immense heat
* Oil Leaks — Check around valve covers and seals for leaks
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate ignition, timing, turbo or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures (V12 emissions are stringent)
* Transferable Warranty — Check if the vehicle still has a transferable warranty — G65 repairs are expensive

items.question: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz G65 AMG engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned G65 AMG engine typically costs £8,000–£12,000 supply only. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 depending on the specialist. A used low‑mileage M279.982 engine is available from specialist suppliers, typically supplied as a bare unit. A full engine replacement can easily exceed £15,000 with labour. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your G‑Class.

items.question: Is the Mercedes‑Benz G65 AMG engine reliable?
items.answer: The M279 V12 is a handcrafted masterpiece derived from the legendary M275 family that powered the S 65 AMG and Pagani Huayra. However, it has well‑documented vulnerabilities. The twin‑turbo V12 generates immense heat, and ignition coils are exposed to extreme thermal loads, leading to premature failure. Turbo bypass valve leaks, timing chain tensioner wear, and cooling system degradation are also common concerns. With proper maintenance and proactive attention to known issues, the M279 can be a rewarding engine — but it requires deep pockets and careful ownership.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz G65 AMG?
items.answer: The most common issues are ignition coil and coil pack failure (misfires, rough running — caused by heat exposure from the twin‑turbo V12), turbocharger bypass valve leaks (power loss, unusual "whirring" noise), timing chain tensioner wear (rattling on cold start), cooling system degradation (hoses, coolant, water pump), and oil leaks from valve cover gaskets and seals. The V12 has twelve individual ignition coils — replacing all twelve as a set is recommended when one fails.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a G65 AMG engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the vehicle value and condition. Against a well‑kept G65 AMG worth £60,000–£120,000+, a £10,000–£12,000 recon is 10–15% of the car's value — often worth it for a cherished example. The G65 AMG is a rare collector's piece, and engine replacement is a significant but justifiable investment for a well‑maintained example. Against a tired example with significant body or drivetrain issues, the same recon may be less justifiable — get a specialist valuation first.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz G65 AMG engine code?
items.answer: The engine code is M279.982, found on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation. It's the 6.0‑litre V12 biturbo fitted to the G65 AMG W463 from 2012–2018. The engine is handcrafted by AMG and produces 621‑630 hp. The 2015+ models received a power upgrade to 630 PS (621 hp).

items.question: Can I repair the G65 ignition coils, or do they need replacement?
items.answer: The ignition coils must be replaced — they are a wear item on the M279 due to the extreme heat from the twin‑turbo V12. A full set of twelve ignition coils costs £800–£2,000. Replacing all twelve as a set is recommended when one fails, as the others are likely to follow. Ignoring a misfire can damage the catalytic converters — an even more expensive repair. At the first sign of misfires or rough running, book an inspection immediately.
